||stringent effuent fnes are imposed by legislation.||
|||& 2007 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.|

## 1. Introduction

Water pollution represents one of the most serious environmental problems due to the discharge of nutrients into receiving waters. Hence, stricter standards for the operation of wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) have been imposed by authorities. In order to meet these standards, improved control of WWTPs is needed. WWTPs should be controlled in such a way that plant operating costs (OC) are minimized, while standards are still maintained.

Different control algorithms for WWTPs have been introduced over the years. For instance, sufficient nitrification can be maintained by applying a constant aeration flow rate, by control of the dissolved oxygen (DO) level at a pre-selected set-point or by using a variable DO set-point controller based on ammonia concentration in the last aerated reactor of the

plant (Ingildsen, 2002; Vrecˇko et al., 2003). On the other hand, the denitrification process is usually controlled by manipulating the external carbon flow rate or internal recirculation flow rate based on nitrate concentration in the last anoxic reactor or in the last aerobic reactor (Lindberg, 1997; Yuan et al., 2002). In recent years different control algorithms have been proposed, from simple ON/OFF and PI control (Ayesa et al., 2006) to complex model predictive control (MPC) (Steffens and Lant, 1999). Unfortunately, various plant configurations, influent characteristics and evaluation criteria have been used in the assessment of control algorithms. As all of these factors influence the choice of a control strategy, it is difficult to say which control algorithm is the most appropriate with respect to minimal OC and best effluent quality, and whether the implementation of complex control algorithms is really necessary.

> �Corresponding author. Tel.: +386 1 47 73 798; fax: +386 1 42 57 009. E-mail address: aljaz.stare@ijs.si (A. Stare). 0043-1354/$ - see front matter & 2007 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. doi:10.1016/j.watres.2007.01.029

2005

WATER RESEARCH 41 (2007) 2004– 2014

This paper presents several control strategies for nitrogen removal that were designed and tested on a benchmark simulation model of an activated sludge process (ASP). Control strategies differ in the information used about the process (i.e. the number of sensors and sensor location) and in the complexity of the control algorithms. Constant manipulated variables and PI and feedforward (FF) controllers were tested, as well as an advanced MPC controller, which was used as a reference for the other control strategies. In this way the control strategy that produces optimal performance regarding OC and yields satisfactory removal of nutrients can be found.

The paper is organized as follows: In the following section a benchmark simulation model is presented. Then the applied control strategies are described, followed by set-point analysis. Next, the presented control strategies are assessed and compared in terms of OC at various plant operating conditions. Finally, the most important conclusions are drawn.

steady state by simulating the plant with the defined constant influent file over a 150-day period. Then, the plant simulation continues by first applying 14 days of the dry influent weather file, followed by 14 days of dry, rain or storm influent file. The performance of the benchmark is then evaluated for the last 7 days of dynamic data.

## 2.1.3. Evaluation criteria

Various criteria have been defined in the benchmark (Copp, 2002) to assess the performance of the plant. In this work, OC that combine aeration costs, sludge disposal costs, external carbon dosage costs and effluent fines were used to evaluate control strategies, without taking into account the investment costs for the implementation of the control strategy (sensors, actuators). Within the benchmark, pumping costs are also defined, but were not considered in our case, as all the pumping flow rates were kept constant. The operating costs [h/d] were calculated as follows:

**==> picture [241 x 10] intentionally omitted <==**

## 2. Materials and methods

## 2.1. Benchmark simulation model

A benchmark simulation model of an ASP was developed by working group No. 1. within COST Actions 624 and 682. The benchmark is a simulation protocol defining a plant layout, a process model, influent data, test procedures and evaluation criteria (Copp, 2002).

## 2.1.1. Plant layout and process model

The benchmark represents a pre-denitrification plant with two anoxic and three aerated compartments (Copp, 2002). However, in our case one anoxic and four aerobic reactors were used (Fig. 1) in order to achieve lower effluent ammonia concentrations. The IWA activated sludge model No. 1 (ASM1) is used to describe the biological processes in the reactors. A further description of ASM1 can be found in Henze et al. (2000). The secondary settler is modelled as a non-reactive, 10-layer process with a double exponential settling velocity model proposed by Taka´cs et al. (1991).

## 2.1.2. Influent data and test procedures

The benchmark influent data include three influent files for three different weather conditions: dry, rain and storm. Each of these files contains 14 days of data at 15-min intervals. To calculate benchmark performance the plant is first run to

where AE is aeration energy, SP is sludge production for disposal, EC is external carbon addition, while EF means effluent fines. The weights g1, g2 and g3 were set in proportion to the weights in the operating cost index (OCI) in the benchmark. The OCI weights (g1, g2 and g3) were defined by the benchmark group and were set according to the relative contribution of AE, SP and EC to operating costs. Based on the experience gained from the WWTPs operation they were chosen to be 1, 5 and 3, respectively. In our case, a rough estimate of average electricity price in EU (0.1 h/kWh) was also taken into account and thus all the weights were multiplied by 0.1. Hence, the final values for g1, g2 and g3 were 0.1 h/kWh, 0.5 h/kg and 0.3 h/kg, respectively.

Average aeration energy (AE [kWh/d]) is calculated for the last 7 days of dynamic data (T). The equation suggested in Copp (2002) was improved by also including the volumes of the aerated reactors (Jeppsson, 2005):

**==> picture [241 x 47] intentionally omitted <==**

where KLai is the oxygen transfer rate (d[�][1] ) in the individual aerated reactor, Vi is the volume of the ith reactor and Vref is 1333 m[3] .

Sludge production (SP) for disposal (kg/d) is calculated based on the amount of total suspended solids in the wastage

**==> picture [310 x 103] intentionally omitted <==**

Fig. 1 – Plant layout.

2006

WAT E R R E S E A R C H 41 (20 07) 2 00 4 – 2 01 4

(MTSSw) that is removed from the plant, and the solids accumulated in the system during the last 7 days of operation:

**==> picture [240 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

The amount of solids in the system (MTSSsystem) is equal to the amount of solids in the reactors (MTSSreactors) and the amount of solids in the settler (MTSSsettler). MTSSw is calculated from XS,w, XBH,w, XBA,w, XP,w and XI,w, which represent concentrations of slowly biodegradable substrate, active heterotrophic and autotrophic biomass, particulate products arising from biomass decay and particulate inert organic matter in the waste sludge, respectively.

External carbon (EC) addition [kg/d] is calculated as the average external carbon mass flow:

**==> picture [240 x 21] intentionally omitted <==**

where CODS is the carbon source concentration (4 � 10[5] mg/l) and Qcar is the carbon flow rate (m[3] /d).

Effluent fines (EF) are usually paid in proportion to the discharge of pollution into receiving waters. In our study, only effluent ammonia (SNHeff) and total nitrogen (STNeff) were considered in the calculation of effluent fines. The reason for this is that the controllers applied in this paper do not have much impact on effluent COD and TSS, which are usually included in the effluent fines. It should be noted that omitting COD and TSS from the EF calculations results in a greater relative difference between the controllers, while the absolute difference between the controllers remains approximately the same. However, as it is quite difficult to remove all the nitrogen components in the benchmark plant, the effluent nitrogen fines are much higher compared to the fines for discharging COD and TSS, which means that also a relative difference between the controllers is not much different despite omitting COD and TSS from the EF calculations.

**==> picture [214 x 172] intentionally omitted <==**

The cost function for EF which was used in our case is shown in Fig. 2 (Carstensen, 1994; Vanrolleghem et al., 1996). Da and Db are costs per kilogram of discharge below and above the effluent limit concentration Climit, while b0 is the cost for exceeding the effluent limit. A complete mathematical description of the cost functions is:

**==> picture [240 x 36] intentionally omitted <==**

where Ceff and Qeff are the effluent concentration and flow rate, respectively. In our case the following values of cost function parameters are used. When effluent ammonia and total nitrogen are below the discharge limit, the costs of discharge are DaNH ¼ 4 h/kg and DaTN ¼ 2.7 h/kg, respectively. When the discharge limits are exceeded, the costs of discharge increase threefold (DbNH ¼ 12 h/kg and DbTN ¼ 8.1 h/kg). The costs of exceeding the discharge limits are b0,NH ¼ 2.7 h/1000 m[3] and b0,TN ¼ 1.4 h/1000 m[3] . Discharge limits for ammonia (SNHlimit) and total nitrogen (STNlimit) were set to 4 and 12 mg/l, respectively, in our case. Note that ammonia is penalized twice, as ammonia and also as part of total nitrogen.

## 2.2. Applied control strategies

In this paper the following manipulated variables were used in the control strategies: external carbon flow rate (Qcar), DO set-point (Soset) and oxygen transfer rate (KLa), which is related to the airflow rate. Other manipulated variables such as internal recycle flow rate (Qa), external recycle flow rate (Qr) and waste sludge flow rate (Qw) were set to the constant values of 55,338, 18,446 and 300 m[3] /d, respectively.

Boundaries of the manipulated variables were the same as in the benchmark. The maximum external carbon flow rate was limited to 5 m[3] /d, with a COD concentration of 4 � 10[5] mg/l, while the oxygen transfer rate (KLa) values were limited between 0 and 360 d[�][1] . The upper bound of the oxygen set-point was limited to 3 mg/l in order to reduce unnecessary aeration of the aerobic reactors. Because of the constraints of the manipulated variables, anti-windup protection, similar to that used in the benchmark, was applied in the PI controllers. A short description of the applied control strategies is given in the following paragraphs.

## 2.2.1. Constant manipulated variables

The most basic control strategy, which is still used in practice, does not contain feedback loops. It simply uses constant manipulated variables. In our case, constant KLa and Qcar were applied as shown in Fig. 3a.

## 2.2.2. Oxygen PI control

Oxygen PI control (Fig. 3b) uses four PI controllers that control DO concentrations in the aerobic reactors at the desired setpoint by manipulating the KLa values. This control strategy needs four additional DO sensors, which are the most commonly used in practice for online control or monitoring. Since the DO sensors are proven to be robust and reliable with relatively fast response times, the benchmark DO sensors are

Fig. 2 – Cost function for effluent fines.

2007

WATER RESEARCH 41 (2007) 2004– 2014

**==> picture [310 x 196] intentionally omitted <==**

Fig. 3 – Control structures: (a) constant manipulated variables, (b) oxygen PI control, (c) nitrate and ammonia PI control, (d) nitrate PI and ammonia FF-PI control, and (e) model predictive control

assumed to be ideal with no delay and noise. In full scale implementation the DO controller is cascaded with the airflow controller in order to avoid nonlinearities in valve operation. The inner controller that receives the airflow rate set-point and controls valve position is not used in the benchmark since it does not influence the final results due to fast valve response.

## 2.2.3. Nitrate and ammonia PI control

In this control strategy, two additional control loops are applied (Fig. 3c). In the first loop, nitrate concentration in the last aerobic reactor (SNO5 ) is controlled with the PI controller that manipulates the external carbon flow rate (Qcar). In the second loop, ammonia concentration in the last aerobic reactor (SNH5 ) is controlled with a cascade controller. The outer PI controller in the cascade control adjusts the DO set-point value (Soset) based on the desired and actual SNH values, and the inner PI controllers manipulate KLa values based on the desired and actual DO values. To apply this control strategy in practice, additional sensors for ammonia and nitrate concentrations are needed. These sensors are not as reliable and responsive as DO sensors, for example. Hence, more realistic SNH and SNO sensor models are included within the benchmark. Both sensors are assumed to have a time delay of 10 min with normally distributed white zero-mean noise. The standard deviation of the SNO and SNH sensors is set to 0.1 and 0.2 mg/l, respectively.

## 2.2.4. Nitrate PI and ammonia feedforward-PI control

The idea of FF control is to act on the process when disturbances appear and before they cause deterioration of the effluent quality. In the proposed strategy, the ammonia cascade PI controller was upgraded with a FF control that uses influent flow rate (Qin) and influent ammonia concentration (SNHin) as measurable disturbances. The control scheme of nitrate PI and ammonia FF-PI control is shown in Fig. 3d.

The DO set-point is in this case calculated as the sum of PI and FF control:

**==> picture [241 x 9] intentionally omitted <==**

where SoPI is the output of the ammonia PI controller and SoFF is the output of the FF controller. The response of the FF controller is proportional to the product of SNHin and Qin and was chosen to be non-zero only during high influent loads:

**==> picture [241 x 25] intentionally omitted <==**

High influent flow rates reduce hydraulic retention times and therefore it is important to increase DO concentrations fast enough to counteract the influent disturbances. During low influent loads the PI controller is sufficiently fast to reject small influent disturbances, and hence the FF part can be switched off. The FF part is switched on/off when the influent nitrogen load (QinSNHin) is higher/lower than the threshold value nload, which was in our case set to a relatively high value of 1000 kg/d (e.g., the average influent nitrogen load in the benchmark is approximately 550 kg/d). The proportional factor k in (7) was set to a value of 2 � 10[�][2] (mgO2 d)/(kgN l). In this way the Soset is increased by 2 mg/l when the influent load reaches 1100 kg/d. For this control strategy the same SNH and SNO sensor models were applied as in the case of nitrate and ammonia PI control.

## 2.2.5. Model predictive control

In order to determine if a more advanced control strategy can further reduce OC and improve effluent quality, a multivariable and nonlinear MPC was implemented. The idea of MPC is to calculate a control sequence by minimizing a certain cost function. The control sequence is calculated at each time step based on set-points, a process model, measured disturbances and outputs (Maciejowski, 2002). In our case, the whole benchmark model with perfect measurements (also for ammonia and nitrate) was used as the process model of the predictive controller, and all influent

2008

WAT E R R E S E A R C H 41 (20 07) 2 00 4 – 2 01 4

disturbances were assumed to be known in advance over the future prediction horizon. This ‘‘ideal’’ control algorithm was used as a reference for the other control algorithms presented above and should represent the upper limit of what can be achieved by predictive control.

The general cost function used in the MPC algorithm can be written as follows:

**==> picture [240 x 79] intentionally omitted <==**

where k denotes the sampling instants, y^ ðk þ iÞ is a vector of the predicted output values, r(k+i) is a vector of the future set-point values, Du(k+i) is a vector of the future input changes, u(k+i) is an input vector, e(k+i) is a slack variable, Hp is the prediction horizon, Hu is the control horizon, Q is a weighting matrix to penalize the error between the predicted process output and the set-point, RDu is a weighting matrix to penalize changes in the control signal, Ru is a weighting matrix to penalize deviations of the input vector from the desired steady-state value (u0), while r is a weight to penalize soft constraint violations.

In our case, ammonia (SNH5 ) and the sum of ammonia and nitrate concentrations (SNH5 þ SNO5 ) in the last aerobic reactor were used as controlled variables, while the manipulated variables were DO set-point (Soref) and external carbon flow rate (Qcar). Input and output vectors can be written as follows:

**==> picture [240 x 25] intentionally omitted <==**

The control scheme of the MPC is shown in Fig. 3e.

control performance is achieved (Maciejowski, 2002). In our case, the parameters were tuned based on experience gained from the simulations and from the tuning rules presented in Maciejowski (2002). Since the dynamics of nutrient removal processes are on a time scale of hours, the prediction horizon was set to 1.5 h, which is 6 time steps (each time step was 15 min). In order to simplify the calculation of the input sequences, only one control move Du was optimized at each sampling instant (Hu ¼ 1), i.e. the control signal was constant during the prediction horizon. The weighting matrix Q was set to diag[10 0] so that only the deviation of SNH5 from the desired set-point was penalized. A soft constraint on SNH5 was also used to additionally penalize concentrations above the effluent limit. This soft constraint was implemented by using a so-called ‘‘slack variable’’, which is defined in such a way that it is non-zero only when the constraint is violated, i.e. when SNH5 values exceed 4 mg/l. The other controlled variable (SNH5 +SNO5 ) was considered in the cost function only with the soft constraint, which was set to 10 mg/l. In this way the aim was to keep SNH5 +SNO5 below 10 mg/l, which could actually be achieved by keeping the value of SNH5 +SNO5 at the desired setpoint of 10 mg/l. However, when SNH5 +SNO5 is below 10 mg/l, no effort is put into increasing SNH5 +SNO5 concentration, as would be the case with set-point tracking. To prevent external carbon from being unnecessarily dosed during low loads (i.e. when the SNH5 +SNO5 concentration is below 10 mg/l) the weighting matrix Ru was set to diag[0 1]. In addition, changes of the manipulated variables were penalized with weighting matrix RDu, which was set to diag[1 1]. This has the effect of reducing the oscillations of the control signals. The parameters of the MPC can be summarized as follows:

**==> picture [239 x 44] intentionally omitted <==**

## 2.3. Tuning of controllers

## 2.3.1. Tuning of PI controllers

Parameters of PI controllers were tuned from the step response experiments using the internal model control (IMC) tuning rules (Olsson and Newell, 1999). The values of the parameters of PI controllers are shown in Table 1.

## 2.3.2. Tuning of the MPC

Parameters of the prediction and control horizon, the weights and the set-point trajectory of the MPC affect the closed-loop behaviour of the plant. The weights in the cost function may be set according to the economic objectives of the plant, but usually they are adjusted in such a way that satisfactory

|---|---|---|---|---|
|||Oxygen PI<br>controller|Ammonia PI<br>controller|Nitrate PI<br>controller|
|Kp||100|�0.3|�0.7|
|Ti||0.01 d|0.2 d|0.1 d|
||||||

## 3. Results

## 3.1. Set-point analysis

The performance of the plant strongly depends on the selected controller set-points. In order to study the impact of the controller set-points on plant performance, a number of dynamic simulations with different set-point values were performed. With such an analysis, operational maps were constructed (see Figs. 4–6) to determine optimal operating points with respect to OC for each control strategy. In the figures the maximum values of the effluent ammonia and effluent total nitrogen concentrations are also plotted in order to see how successfully the controllers reject influent disturbances. The obtained optimal set-points with respect to OC are shown in Table 2.

## 3.2. Comparison of control strategies at optimal set-point values

Each control strategy was simulated using the optimal setpoint values given in Table 2. The results of simulations from

2009

WATER RESEARCH 41 (2007) 2004– 2014

**==> picture [462 x 436] intentionally omitted <==**

the 8th to the 10th day of influent data are shown in Figs. 7–9. From Fig. 7 (left) it can be seen that when constant manipulated variables are used, the removal of ammonia is not efficient. In this case the aerobic reactors are underaerated during high influent loads (Fig. 8, left), leading to poor ammonia removal, and over-aerated during low loads, leading to unnecessarily high DO concentrations and energy consumption in these periods (Fig. 8, right). With an oxygen PI controller that controls DO concentrations at the desired constant set-point, the plant is better controlled with respect to energy consumption, but still under-aerated during high influent loads. Considerably better removal of ammonia is achieved by using control strategies where ammonia concentrations are controlled (Fig. 7, left). In these cases, the ammonia controller increases DO concentrations during high

load periods (see Fig. 8, right) to enhance nitrification and to remove ammonia peaks more successfully, while during low loads it decreases the DO concentrations to save aeration energy. In addition, low DO set-points also enable simultaneous denitrification in the aerobic reactors, leading to lower total nitrogen concentrations (Fig. 7, right). In the case of ammonia PI and ammonia FF-PI control, the maximum effluent SNH peak is reduced to 6 and 4 mg/l, respectively. The differences between these two cases with respect to ammonia peak removal are quite significant. With an ammonia PI controller the DO concentrations are not increased during high influent loads until SNH5 is high. Hence, while ammonia concentrations in the first three aerobic reactors are high, considering the propagation of the influent load through the reactors, the nitrification rate is still low 2010 WAT E R R E S E A R C H 41 (20 07) 2 00 4 – 2 01 4 **==> picture [306 x 220] intentionally omitted <==** **----- Start of picture text -----**

Table 2 – Optimal set-point values for each control strategy |strategy|strategy||| |---|---|---|---| |||Optimal values of set-points|| |Constant manipulated||KLa¼150 d�1, Qcar¼2.2 m3/d|| |variables|||| |Oxygen PI control||Soset¼0.53 mg/l, Qcar¼1.6 m3/d|| |Nitrate and ammonia PI||SNH5set ¼2:0 mg=l , SNO5set ¼9:0 mg=l|| |control|||| |Nitrate PI and ammonia||SNH5set ¼2:0 mg=l, SNO5set ¼8:8 mg=l|| |FF-PI control|||| |MPC||SNH5set ¼2:0 mg=l|| ||||| because of low DO concentrations. With ammonia FF-PI control, a better reduction of ammonia peaks is achieved because the DO concentrations are increased more than 1 h earlier (Fig. 8, right), which leads to better removal of SNH. In the case of MPC the algorithm increases DO concentrations even a little earlier and hence further improvements with respect to ammonia removal are obtained. Even though the upper limit for DO concentration was 3 mg/l, none of the controllers could manage to remove the effluent ammonia peaks completely. The problem is that the aeration volumes of the benchmark are too small for the N load applied to the plant, and should be increased in order to achieve complete ammonia removal. An improvement in ammonia removal could be also achieved by increasing the sludge age (i.e. reducing the waste sludge flow rate). However, it should be mentioned that the default benchmark value of Qw was already reduced from 385 to 300 m[3] /d in our case. Therefore, even lower Qw value can cause a significant increase in the effluent suspended solids concentrations as a result of higher mixed liquor suspended solids (MLSS) concentration and thus increased solids loading to the settler. This can be especially evident during heavy rainfall and storm events as these have an important effect on the hydraulics of a treatment plant and may disturb the final settling to such an extent that sludge wash-out occurs. Comparison of carbon addition flow rates (Fig. 9) shows that with the constant carbon addition flow rates (in the case of constant manipulated variables and oxygen PI control) similar problems occur as with constant aeration, i.e. too much external carbon is unnecessarily added during low influent loads, while too little carbon is added during high influent loads. External carbon can be dosed more efficiently with a PI controller that adds external carbon only when the nitrate concentration in the fifth reactor is high in order to enhance denitrification. The MPC controller starts to change the external carbon dosing much earlier than the nitrate PI controller, and therefore a better reduction of effluent total nitrogen peaks is achieved (Fig. 7, right). Fig. 7 shows that during high influent loads the removal of STNeff peaks is much better with constant manipulated variables and oxygen PI control than with nitrate PI control. Namely, in the first two cases, low DO concentrations during high influent loads enable simultaneous denitrification in aerobic reactors, leading to lower STNeff. However, during low influent loads the STNeff concentrations are much higher in these cases because of high DO concentrations. Control strategies were also evaluated with the criteria described in Section 2.1.3. The obtained values of the evaluation criteria are given in Table 3. The control strategy with constant manipulated variables gives the largest OC and is used as a reference case for comparison. It can be seen that with each control strategy introduced the OC are further reduced. An important factor contributing to lower OC are lower effluent fines, which constantly decrease with each control strategy introduced. Similarly, the costs of carbon addition greatly decrease with the introduction of nitrate controllers, but slightly increase in the case of MPC because more carbon is needed for better total nitrogen removal. OC is reduced by about 7% when oxygen PI control is used, amounting to around 203 h per day (or 74,000 h per year), which is reasonably high taking into account that only simple oxygen PI controllers have to be used. Further cost reductions of 2.5% (74 h per day) are achieved by using nitrate and ammonia PI control. With nitrate PI control and ammonia FFPI control, OC are reduced by an additional 3% (85 h per day). In the case of MPC, only a slight improvement in OC is achieved (around 1% or 25 h per day). (h/d)|3249|3058 (�5.9%)|2895 (�10.9%)|2810 (�13.5%)|2773 (�14.7%)| ||||||| fines are reduced considerably, but OC increase because of the greater addition of external carbon. ## 3.3. Performance of controllers at different influent and temperature conditions Besides the comparison of the best possible performance of the controllers, it is of great interest to study how the controllers perform under different operating conditions. In reality, WWTPs are subjected to different seasonal effects (i.e. temperature changes, influent composition and concentrations variations, different sludge properties, etc.), which means that controller set-points that were optimal at certain plant conditions are suboptimal under other conditions. Therefore, this section investigates the performance of different controllers if the plant loading conditions and temperature change. To change the plant loading conditions, SNHin, SSin and Qin were multiplied by a factor 1.15 or 0.85 during the last 7 days of dry influent data, thus increasing or decreasing the plant’s average influent load and its dynamics. In addition, the rain and storm conditions as defined in the benchmark were studied as well. Temperature was changed to 121 and 181 for the whole simulation period by adjusting the activated sludge kinetic parameters (mH, bH, mA, bA, kh and ka) through an Arrhenius function. At 151 the kinetic parameters are equal to the default benchmark parameters. In the analysis the controller set-points were set to the same values obtained under the default benchmark conditions (Table 2). In Table 4 the control algorithms are compared with respect to OC for different plant conditions. For each control strategy, the percentage change of OC compared to the case of constant manipulated variables is also calculated. The percentages are shown for each individual change in plant operating conditions, as well as for the average OC calculated from all cases studied (bottom line in Table 4). In virtually all cases the results show that OC decreased with the introduction of new control strategies, the only exception being oxygen PI control at 12 1C, where OC increased compared to constant manipulated variables because of a low DO set-point. The differences between nitrate and ammonia PI control, nitrate PI and ammonia FF-PI, and MPC are more obvious at higher influent loads than at lower loads, while the improvements with MPC are rather small. From the study it can be concluded that more advanced controllers result in lower OC over a wider range of plant operating conditions and also when the controller set-points are not chosen optimally for a given plant’s operating conditions. From Table 4 it can be seen that the reduction of OC depends on plant operating conditions (influent load, temperature), but on average the reduction of costs with more advanced control strategies is even slightly higher than at the default benchmark conditions in Table 3. This can be explained by the fact that with the ammonia and nitrate controllers the change of operating 2013 WATER RESEARCH 41 (2007) 2004– 2014 conditions acts as a disturbance and the controllers themselves adjust the low-level set-points (DO set-point, KLa, external carbon flow rate), while in the case of constant manipulated variables and oxygen control these variables are kept constant despite process changes. In these two cases the choice of optimal set-points is more sensitive to process changes. ## 4. Discussion The results presented here show that with nitrate PI and ammonia FF-PI control almost the same OC can be achieved as with advanced MPC algorithms. Only when the plant is highly loaded would an MPC algorithm be advantageous. It should be emphasized that the MPC used in the study was ideal with respect to the exact process model, all measurements available and perfect knowledge of the disturbances. It can be expected that any MPC implementation in a real system would result in smaller differences with respect to OC and effluent quality. Hence, it appears that additional information about the plant, along with an advanced multivariable and nonlinear control algorithm, would not improve the operation of the plant to such an extent that the investment in buying additional sensors and implementing more complex control algorithms would pay off. The improvement is thus more related to control structure, i.e. where the sensors and the actuators are located, than to the control algorithms. Similar conclusions were also arrived at in a simulation study by Lukasse and Keesman (1999), where a simple feedback controller based on easily implemented rules was designed to closely imitate the optimal operating strategy for an alternating ASP. However, it should also be kept in mind that the results presented are based on a simulation model, which is only an approximation of the real process. Therefore, full-scale experiments would also be needed in order to additionally support the conclusions. In general, the optimal OC for each control strategy strongly depends on established effluent fines, i.e. selected parameters of Da, Db and b0. In our case the cost of discharge increased threefold each time the discharge limits were exceeded (Fig. 2), which favours control algorithms that keep the effluent concentrations away from their defined limits. However, if a constant effluent fine per kg of pollutant discharged over the complete effluent concentration range is used (b0 ¼ 0, Db ¼ Da), it turns out that smaller improvements can be obtained with advanced control algorithms. For example, under the default benchmark conditions the optimal OC and percentage improvement compared to constant manipulated variables are 2691 h/d, 2613 h/d (�2.9%), 2559 h/d (�4.9%), 2536 h/d (�5.8%) and 2525 h/d (�6.2%) for the constant manipulated variables, oxygen PI control, nitrate and ammonia PI control, nitrate PI and ammonia FF-PI control, and MPC, respectively. The results also indicate that, in this case, the optimal set-points lie in a region where Qcar is zero, and thus due to softer effluent fines external carbon should not be added in order to minimize OC (carbon costs, sludge production costs as well as aeration costs). On the other hand, heavier effluent fines would favour advanced control algorithms even more. While a study such as this one can show what is possible to achieve in the operation of WWTPs, the actual implementation of advanced control strategies strongly depends on the effluent fines imposed by legislation. For sensitive discharge points it is important to prevent high peak values from occurring, and therefore it is better to implement advanced control algorithms. However, for less sensitive water bodies, peak discharge will not be so important and hence it is probably sufficient to use simple control algorithms. In the study the same sensors that are defined within the benchmark were applied. However, a number of sensors with different performance characteristics are commercially available, which means that in reality it is vital to use sensors with short response times and low measurement noise as the control performance strongly depends on these sensor characteristics. For example, if SNH and SNO sensors with even longer response times and higher measurement noise were used, the difference between the oxygen PI control algorithm and the nitrate and ammonia PI control algorithm would be even smaller, while the usage of FF controller would be more favourable in this case. On the other hand, SNH and SNO PI control algorithm would achieve almost optimal performance regarding OC if sensors with shorter response times and lower measurement noise were used as the proportional gain of PI controllers can be increased, which leads to faster controller response and hence better rejection of influent disturbances. It should also be noted that the investment and maintenance costs are higher in the case of complex control algorithms because of a large number of actuators and sensors required. In reality actuators and sensors are also subjected to failure, which means that control algorithms that use a large number of actuators and sensors are also more vulnerable to malfunctions. These kinds of problems should be solved with additional fault detection algorithms that detect errors and failures and that initiate proper steps to correct them. As detection and accommodation of failures takes time the advanced control algorithms will consequently provide optimal behaviour for a shorter period of time. In our study, the evaluation of control strategies based on such malfunctions was not considered. From the study it can be seen that simple feedback controllers are often sufficient to reach improved plant performance with respect to OC, while the MPC algorithm could not deliver much better results despite the fact that additional information about the process was used. Hence, it is unlikely that with the existing manipulated variables (KLa, Soset, Qcar) one can achieve much greater reduction of OC. Some further improvements could be expected with the use of additional manipulated variables such as internal recycle flow rate, external recycle flow rate and waste sludge flow rate. However, the main reason that greater reduction of OC cannot be achieved is due to the plant configuration with relatively small total reactor volumes for a given influent load. To avoid such a problem, the integrated process design and control may be used, which aims at identifying design decisions that would potentially generate and inherit possible trouble to the dynamic performance (Seferlis and Georgiadis, 2004). In our case only one anoxic reactor within the given total reactors volume was used, which limits the denitrification process. On the other hand, if two anoxic reactors had been used, the nitrification process would have been limited. Therefore, the introduction of the aeration 2014 WAT E R R E S E A R C H 41 (20 07) 2 00 4 – 2 01 4 volume control (i.e., the control of the distribution between the aerobic and anoxic reactor volumes) may be an interesting subject for further research. ## 5. Conclusions In this paper several control strategies for nitrogen removal in an ASP were evaluated using a benchmark simulation model. The aim of the study was to investigate the differences between the controllers in terms of plant OC. In the study, constant manipulated variables, simple PI and FF controllers, as well as a more advanced MPC controller, were evaluated. The controllers differed in the information used about the process and in the complexity of the control algorithms. For each control strategy, an operational map was constructed in order to obtain optimal set-point values. The study shows that control strategies that use on-line nitrate and ammonia measurements give better performance with respect to OC than control algorithms with constant manipulated variables or oxygen control. They are also less sensitive to the optimal choice of controller set-points and can be valid for a wider range of plant operating conditions. While control strategies with more complex control structures and algorithms yield constant and significant improvement in plant performance with respect to effluent fines, they also result in higher OC (aeration costs, carbon costs) and therefore their implementation is not always justifiable. In particular, the study shows that with relatively simple PI nitrate and FF-PI ammonia controllers nearly similar OC can be achieved as with more advanced MPC algorithms. More advanced control algorithms are advantageous only when the plant is highly loaded and if stringent effluent fines are imposed by legislation. It is expected that in reality the differences between advanced MPC algorithms and relatively simple PI and FF control algorithms would be even smaller considering that an MPC with a perfect model, perfect measurements and perfect knowledge of the disturbances was used in the study. Hence, the improvement is thus more related to control structure, i.e. where the sensors and the actuators are located, than to the control algorithms.

text:
https://doi.org/10.1038/s44221-024-00316-4 ## **nature water** **Analysis** ## **Valuing energy flexibility from water systems** Received: 29 February 2024 Accepted: 5 September 2024 Published online: 27 September 2024 Check for updates Published online: 27 September 2024 **Akshay K. Rao[1] , Jose Bolorinos[1] , Erin Musabandesu[1] , Fletcher T. Chapin[1] & Meagan S. Mauter[1,2,3,4,5]** Water systems represent an untapped source of electric power load flexibility, but determining the value of this flexibility requires quantitative comparisons to other grid-scale energy storage technologies and a compelling economic case for water system operators. Here we present a unified framework for representing water asset flexibility using grid-scale energy storage metrics (round-trip efficiency, energy capacity and power capacity) and assessing the technoeconomic benefits of energy flexibility at the water facility scale (levelized cost of water and levelized value of flexibility). We apply this framework to case studies of an advanced water treatment (desalination) plant, a water distribution network and a wastewater treatment plant. The framework reveals strengths and limitations of water system flexibility relative to other grid-scale energy storage solutions, high-value opportunities for flexible load operation of water assets and the critical role of electricity tariff structures and energy service markets in determining water sector participation in load flexibility. Ultimately, this unified framework for valuating water asset flexibility enables both electricity and water asset managers to prioritize investments based on levelized cost comparisons across their respective portfolios. Most pathways for decarbonizing the electricity sector by 2050 include substantial increases in intermittent renewable generation paired with several TWh of grid-scale energy storage[1][–][3] . Grid-scale energy storage refers to technologies with the ability to shift the net demand for electricity over time[4] . Options for grid-scale storage technologies broadly include chemical or thermal storage (for example, molten salt, phase change materials), bidirectional electricity storage (for example, lithium-ion, redox flow) and flexible generation or controllable loads (for example, electric vehicles, building systems, water infrastructure and so on)[5] . The value and performance of these diverse sources of grid-scale electricity storage are frequently described in terms of their round-trip efficiency (energy returned after storage), energy capacity (amount of energy that may be stored or shifted), power capacity (the speed at which energy can be charged or discharged) and cost (the cost to install and operate a device over its lifetime)[6] . Other critical metrics include: dispatchability, durability and stability. Grid operators and investors use these metrics to compare diverse technologies, determine the net present value of marginal energy storage and minimize the costs of grid stabilization. Whereas these metrics are readily derived for stand-alone storage technologies (that is, chemical, thermal, bidirectional), we are unaware of a unified framework for characterizing the value of flexible load energy services (that is, virtual batteries) across diverse, multifunctional assets. The closest equivalent is prior work developing analogues between stand-alone storage and flexible thermostatic loads from buildings[7][–][10] A generalizable approach for valuing energy flexibility assets across diverse sectors would assist electric power grid operators in assessing the dispatchability of flexible loads, the potential for demand response to mitigate resource intermittency and the lowest cost pathways for reliable power supply[11][,][12] . A generalizable framework would also support sector-specific efforts to assess the extent and value of load flexibility across asset classes and individual facilities. Finally, asset operators could use this framework to maximize the net present value of flexible load operation by managing their assets to minimize electricity bills or generate revenue through participation in energy services markets. > 1Civil and Environmental Engineering, Stanford University, Stanford, CA, USA. 2Woods Institute for the Environment, Stanford University, Stanford, CA, USA. > 3Precourt Institute for Energy, Stanford University, Stanford, CA, USA. 4Photon Science, SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory, Menlo Park, CA, USA. > 5Environmental Social Sciences, Stanford University, Stanford, CA, USA. e-mail: mauter@stanford.edu Nature Water | Volume 2 | October 2024 | 1028–1037 **1028** **Analysis** https://doi.org/10.1038/s44221-024-00316-4 **==> picture [521 x 311] intentionally omitted <==** **----- Start of picture text -----**

**Fig. 1 | Mechanisms of load flexibility for energy services and bill savings in the water sector. a** , Mechanisms for load flexibility depicted for each stage of the water supply chain. **b** – **d** , Canonical systems diagrams of the case studies described in this study are depicted in advanced water treatment ( **b** ), water distribution ( **c** ) and wastewater treatment ( **d** ). ‘S’ signifies water storage capacity via tanks or natural reservoirs. Lightning bolts within each unit process qualitatively denote relative energy intensity. Shading relates to the concentrations of salt or waste (darker refers to higher concentration). Dotted lines refer to features that are not universal. The need for a generalizable framework is especially acute for valuing flexibility in critical infrastructure assets. Here load flexibility can introduce tension between domain-specific management priorities (for example, product quality, product value, energy service capacity) and cost savings or revenue generation. For example, in the water sector, a hierarchy of management priorities is codified through regulation. Human safety, water quality and system reliability[13] take precedence over minimizing operating costs, generating new revenue streams or minimizing carbon emissions. Providing energy services (for example, demand response, frequency regulation and so on) may advance the worthy goal of enhancing system affordability, but the degree of energy flexibility in the water asset, and the extent to which flexibility is deployed, depend on first meeting water system reliability targets. Assessing and deploying latent flexibility in critical infrastructure assets also requires a generalizable approach for evaluating that flexibility across asset classes and individual facilities. Within the water sector, the differences in the latent flexibility of drinking water treatment, water distribution and wastewater treatment assets are a result of underlying differences in process dynamics, supply chain constraints and asset coupling, the degree of digitization and economic structures. For example, each facility is a network of unit processes with different start-up/shutdown attributes and process dynamics. Coupling within facilities and across assets (for example, treatment plants, reservoirs and so on) can constrain the operation of upstream and downstream operations. The physical and digital systems for start-up, shutdown or modifications to system operation are not always controllable on the sub-hourly timescales that determine electricity prices[14] . And the net financial benefits of load flexibility are highly dependent on the tariff structures and energy services markets in which the facility is operating[15][,][16] . The large variance of these factors across facilities and asset classes has impeded a generalized understanding of the potential for, and value of, load flexibility at water facilities. Despite this variance, the water sector shares key features of other sectors with high-value flexible loads: large aggregate energy consumption, high energy costs and capacity for real-time control[11] . Water and wastewater utilities account for approximately 4% of global electricity demand (0.6–6.2% on a regional basis), though it is uncertain what fraction of this load is flexible[17][,][18] . Energy intensity and electricity costs vary considerably across the water sector[19][,][20] ; electricity typically represents approximately 30–50% of advanced water treatment lifetime costs, 60–80% of distribution and transmission costs and 20–30% of wastewater treatment costs[20][–][22] . Finally, the water sector’s capacity for real-time control has expanded dramatically in recent years, though legacy systems lack the industrial automation equipment required for flexible load operation[23] . We believe that both water asset managers and electric power grid operators would benefit from a unified, quantitative framework for valuing the net benefits of energy flexibility from water assets and comparing that value to other grid-scale energy storage solutions. Any such framework must explicitly account for increased compliance risk for water infrastructure and accommodate diverse operating scenarios and flexibility mechanisms with nonlinear dynamics. It must be scalable to accommodate different facility designs, capacities, network structures and data ingestion and representation approaches. It must accommodate a variety of economic data such as electricity Nature Water | Volume 2 | October 2024 | 1028–1037 **1029** **Analysis** https://doi.org/10.1038/s44221-024-00316-4 **==> picture [521 x 174] intentionally omitted <==** **----- Start of picture text -----**

**Fig. 2 | Information flow.** The proposed approach to characterize and value flexible operating schema in terms of a baseline operating schedule and economic data. _Ė_ denotes the power consumption, or electrical load, of the facility; and _Q_ denotes the target flow rate (water treatment, water production or water delivery) associated with the given system. tariff structures, capital upgrades and depreciating assets. Finally, the framework must align with existing decision-making frameworks that are used by both grid and water system operators. This paper begins by reviewing the forms of load flexibility that are available to water systems, with a particular focus on desalination (advanced water treatment), water distribution and wastewater treatment. Next, we introduce a set of equations for calculating the round-trip efficiency, energy capacity, power capacity, the net present value of flexibility and the levelized cost of flexibility in water facilities. These metrics may be applied to any water facility, irrespective of the scale of the facility, original facility design, downstream supply chain constraints or resolution of measurements. We then demonstrate the calculation of these metrics using simplified energy models of a seawater desalination plant, a water distribution network and a wastewater treatment plant and using an existing tariff structure from Santa Barbara Clean Energy. We also perform scenario analysis using existing electricity rates from water utilities in California, Texas, Florida and New York to explore the effects of electricity rate structure design on the value of flexibility. Finally, we discuss the implications of our findings on the growth potential for load flexibility resources from the water sector. ## **Mechanisms for energy flexibility in water assets** The water sector has a diverse set of mechanisms for delivering energy flexibility (Fig. 1a). In water transmission infrastructure, operators can modulate energy load by switching between different raw water sources with different energy intensities or by temporarily turning off pumps and relying on stored water[22] . In drinking water infrastructure (Fig. 1b), load can be dynamically controlled by modulating the water recovery or treatment intensity[13][,][24][,][25] . Supply curtailment can also be used to temporarily reduce, within safety limits, the amount of water that is sent to the distribution network or storage[26][,][27] . The water distribution system can leverage extensive water storage (Fig. 1c) to minimize short-term pumping load on intermittently operated pumps[28][–][31] . Demand curtailment, or efforts to temporarily reduce water consumption through price signals or explicit conservation mandates, are typically used during droughts but could conceivably be used over shorter time durations[32] . Finally, wastewater systems (Fig. 1d) can use dynamic process control to vary volume of treatment flow, aeration and biogas production over time[33][–][35] . Energy flexibility is further enhanced in facilities with on-site biogas generation and cogeneration. The ability to compare the costs and benefits of these diverse flexibility mechanisms across the water system will be essential to their adoption by water system operators. ## **Energy flexibility performance and cost metrics** We propose a framework and associated set of equations for characterizing water system load flexibility using metrics for energy performance that are directly analogous to other grid-scale energy services (Fig. 2). As highlighted in the introduction, key metrics of merit for grid operators include round-trip efficiency, energy capacity (MWh) and power capacity (MW). When combined with economic parameters (for example, accelerated maintenance, electricity cost savings and so on), this framework provides a generalizable approach for valuing this load flexibility in terms familiar to water system operators: net present value, the levelized cost of water and the levelized value of flexibility. ## **Round-trip efficiency** Round-trip efficiency (RTE) ( _η_ RT, %) or energy storage efficiency (equation (1)) is defined as the ratio between the power consumed during the discharging ( _E_ out) and charging ( _E_ in) phases of operation[8][,][12] . **==> picture [207 x 29] intentionally omitted <==** Here _E_ represents the load or power demand from the system, subscripts 0 and _f_ denote the baseline and flexibly operated cases, respectively, and time is represented by _t_ . The time step should be selected to match the rate structure defined in relevant energy tariffs but will most commonly be either 15 min or 1 h. The set _D_ represents all time steps at which the flexibly operating system is effectively ‘discharging’ or consuming less energy than the baseline. Conversely, the set _C_ contains the ‘charging’ time steps and represents the system consuming more than the baseline load. RTE is a dimensionless ratio of energy consumption and is bounded between 0 to 1. In the case where the target flow rate ( _Q_ ) is not equal between flexible and baseline operating schema, this definition of efficiency does not hold, as the system does not complete a round trip. Examples of cases where a round trip may not be completed include water supply curtailment (for example, a desalination plant shutting down and deferring production to an alternative supply) and water demand curtailment (for example, water conservation efforts that reduce the volume of water pumped via a distribution network). ## **Energy capacity** Energy capacity ( _E_ ) represents the amount of energy that can be flexibly shifted and has units of kWh or MWh. The normalized energy capacity represents the fraction of load that is shiftable. Nature Water | Volume 2 | October 2024 | 1028–1037 **1030** **Analysis** https://doi.org/10.1038/s44221-024-00316-4 **==> picture [220 x 23] intentionally omitted <==** **==> picture [166 x 27] intentionally omitted <==** The energy discharged, equation (2), represents the difference between the flexible and baseline load during discharging time steps. This can be written as an integral over the entire time horizon or the set of time steps at which the system is discharging ( _D_ ). The results are normalized to baseline energy consumption over the time horizon in equation (3). It should be noted that the charging and discharging energy are related via the round-trip efficiency. ## **Power capacity** Power capacity ( _P_ ) represents the average rate at which the system can charge or discharge and has units of kilowatts or megawatts. The normalized power capacity represents the average rate at which a load can be curtailed as a fraction of its peak power. **==> picture [191 x 145] intentionally omitted <==** The power capacity can be calculated during the charging time frame (equation (4)), the discharging time frame (equation (5)) or as an average over the entire time horizon (equation (6)). The total power capacity is a time-weighted average of charging ( _C_ ) and discharging ( _D_ ) power capacities. The charging and discharging forms may be useful in specific contexts. For example, demand-response markets incentive facilities to shed load below their baseline rate, and the magnitude of that shed capacity is the discharging power capacity ( _PD_ ). In this study, we analyse the dimensionless form of the total average power by normalizing by the maximum baseline power consumption, (equation (7)). It should be noted that these metrics are defined to be context specific and evaluate the relative performance between baseline and flexible operating schedules at a single facility. ## **Value of flexibility** The net present value (NPV) or value of flexibility is a balance between long-term returns (benefit minus costs) and upfront costs (capital expenditures). **==> picture [214 x 106] intentionally omitted <==** **==> picture [153 x 20] intentionally omitted <==** Costs (equation (8)) account for both additional maintenance costs (∆OM) of flexible operations and the value of lost water production associated with any flow curtailment. The additional maintenance (∆OM) term is defined as the increase in operating and maintenance costs beyond the baseline schedule due to unique operations or accelerated component degradation. This value is zero for assets with native flexibility. The cost of flow curtailment is approximated using the levelized cost of water of the baseline (LCOWbaseline ). Whereas this term equals zero when water production normally occurring during peak hours is fully shifted to off-peak hours (no net water production curtailment), in water systems with diverse supplies, it may be more profitable to curtail net production from high marginal cost plants. In this study, we introduce an intermittent advanced water treatment case that simulates curtailment of 5 h of lost baseline production (21%), which is consistent with the duration of demand-response events[14] . Benefits (equation (9)) include electricity cost savings and ancillary service payouts. The electricity cost savings stem from optimizing power consumption ( _E_ ) relative to baseline operation to avoid periods with high electricity rates. _fe_ is a function that calculates the electricity cost associated with a given load profile and tariff structure[36] . Ancillary service payouts incorporate the expected revenue from participation in programmes or markets such as demand response or frequency regulation[37] . _f_ s represents the function that maps the flexible load profile to the expected payout in a specified programme. Given the stochastic nature of these ancillary service markets, this study sets the ancillary service payout term to zero. For reference, the authors are aware of several instances in which ancillary service contracts with water treatment facilities are returning over 10% of the annual electricity bills in exchange for commitments to six shutdowns per year. The net present value of flexible load operation (equation (10)) is calculated by summing the discounted costs and benefits with any upfront capital costs. The discount rate ( _r_ ) is formulated and applied over _N_ discrete interest periods. Whereas the resolution (usually annual) is not necessarily the same as the simulation time step, the overall absolute time horizon of the interest period and simulation must match. For example, 3% annual depreciation over 30 years would reduce the expected benefit by nearly 35%. Net capital costs of the flexibility investment (CapExf) include facility upgrades required to shift load (for example, variable speed drives, intermediate tank storage, control software) and any revenue associated with selling obsolete equipment during a flexibility retrofit. Finally, the return on investment (ROI) for flexible load operation upgrades (equation (11)) is defined by the net present value of the investment divided by the upfront capital costs. ROI (%) is a normalized metric that aids financial comparisons independent of the scale of the water facility. The flexibility investments are simulated for a representative year within a 30-year lifetime. The value of flexibility metric builds on prior work that assumed a fixed cost of water and solved for the net present value of flexibility at the grid scale[11] . The formulation in this paper accounts for any costs associated with flexibly operating facilities and applies facility-centric, physics-informed models that constrain flexibility to reasonable bounds (for example, target flow-rate requirements, constraints associated with process dynamics). ## **Levelized cost and value** The levelized value of flexibility (LVOF), equation (12), represents the net present value of flexibility to the facility per unit of energy Nature Water | Volume 2 | October 2024 | 1028–1037 **1031** **Analysis** https://doi.org/10.1038/s44221-024-00316-4 discharged, where both NPV and Δ _ED_ are calculated over the facility lifetime. **==> picture [183 x 24] intentionally omitted <==** **==> picture [155 x 8] intentionally omitted <==** **==> picture [196 x 25] intentionally omitted <==** In market equilibrium, the LVOF is equal to the negative of the levelized cost of flexibility (LCOF) (equation (13)). The impact of flexible load operation on the water treatment asset’s levelized cost of water (LCOW) delivery is determined by subtracting the net present value of flexibility from the baseline LCOW (equation (14)). LCOF is directly comparable to the value of other grid-scale storage options on a US$ MWh[−1] basis. Similarly, LCOWflexible enables facile comparison of flexibility investments across the water sector and the impact of flexibility on the levelized cost of water for a specific facility. Finally, an alternative formulation of equation (12) could normalize the NPV by the power capacity ( _P_ ) to get a levelized cost in units of US$ MW[−1] . ## **Operationalizing energy flexibility performance metrics** Whereas baseline operations are optimized to maximize the energy efficiency of the facilities, flexible operations minimize the energy costs of the facility. The peak energy price from the Santa Barbara Clean Energy tariff case is over four times the off-peak energy price (Fig. 5), providing a strong incentive for water asset managers to flexibly operate facilities (Fig. 3a–c). In the continuous desalination case (advanced water treatment, Fig. 3a), the plant shifts load by reducing water recovery during the peak hours and increasing recovery during off-peak hours to meet daily production goals. In the intermittent desalination case (with curtailment), the system halts water production during the peak hours. In the water distribution case (Fig. 3b), water delivery is moved earlier in the day (hours 3–10) to allow the pumps to shut down during the peak hours while still meeting water delivery requirements. In the wastewater treatment case (Fig. 3c), the influent storage tank is allowed to fill during the peak hours, enabling the system to reschedule energy-intensive treatment steps to off-peak hours without violating treatment constraints or overfilling intermediate tanks. Each system exhibits a non-sheddable load or a minimum amount of energy that is required to sustain operations and meet system-specific target flow constraints (that is, daily water treatment minimums, hourly water delivery requirements, sub-hourly influent treatment demands). The technical and economic conditions in the case studies (Fig. 3d–i), generally result in optimized load flexibility with high round-trip efficiency (greater than 85%) but relatively low energy and power capacities (less than 30%). For reference, the round-trip efficiency of the baseline lithium-ion battery is 86%, whereas the energy and power capacities depend on the battery specifications[6] . Systems that are right sized or have tight operational constraints often have low energy and power capacities. Critical water treatment, distribution and wastewater infrastructure is typically both right sized (to minimize costs) and tightly operationally constrained (to maximize safety and reliability). The desalination case with curtailment generally has the highest energy and power capacity, but an undefined round-trip efficiency (see note in definitions section) because the water production target is reduced. As shown by the continuous case of desalination, simply using process control (for example, variable water recovery) as a flexibility mechanism results in approximately three times less energy and power capacity than the intermittent mode of flexibility (Fig. 3d). Normalized capacities (Fig. 3d–i) are adjusted by the respective baseline load to obtain the metrics in absolute units (MWh or MW). For example, a desalination system operating at a constant baseline of 1.8 MW (43 MWh per day) with normalized energy and power capacities of 10% and 20%, respectively, would have an absolute energy and power capacity of 4.3 MWh and 0.9 MW. The electricity tariff structure (for example, demand charges, energy charges and customer charges) strongly influence the optimized flexibility performance characteristics of each system (Fig. 3g–i). For example, the summer weekday tariff structure in Santa Barbara heavily incentivizes load shedding and peak power shaving from 16:00 to 21:00, as shown in Fig. 3a–c. However, in Houston, the time invariant (that is, flat) energy charge disincentivizes deviation from the optimally efficient baseline and results in high round-trip efficiencies and low energy and power capacities. ## **Operationalizing energy flexibility cost metrics**

metadata.paper_id: Valuing energy flexibility from water systems  Nature Water

text:
Combining energy flexibility performance metrics with individual facility and electricity market attributes ultimately informs the net benefits of flexible load operation for water treatment asset managers. The financial benefits of shifting load to avoid high energy and demand charges must be weighed against the additional costs of deploying native asset flexibility. Costs may stem from an increase the total electricity consumed, additional operating and maintenance costs at the facility or modest capital upgrades to sensing and control systems required for flexible load operation. These costs are accounted for within the proposed energy flexibility cost metrics, as discussed in assumptions and modelling approach. We explore the financial implications of flexible operation across water assets as a function of net capital upgrade costs, facility age and tariff structure (Fig. 4). The LVOF of each water asset is a function of the capital upgrade cost and facility lifetime after upgrades (Fig. 4a–d). The LVOF for core water assets is positive above the NPV break-even line and financially preferable to the purchase and installation of a 1-LHE battery system (that is, battery sized to provide power to the facility for 1 h of average baseline facility operations) in cases above the ‘battery’ line. To generalize this case study, we then explore the sensitivity of the native LVOF to different tariff structures assuming a net-zero cost of upgrade and compare this to the value of a 1-LHE on-site battery (Fig. 4e). Finally, we provide the NPV of three diverse scenarios in tabular form (Fig. 4f). Under the Santa Barbara Clean Energy tariff case, all water assets have a positive LVOF when the net capital upgrade costs are zero (Fig. 4a–d). Assets with more flexible loads (that is, high energy capacity) have higher LVOFs because they can shift more load outside peak electricity price periods. The intermittently operating desalination system with curtailment has both the highest normalized energy capacity (Fig. 3d) and the highest LVOF[27] (Fig. 4b). The LVOF is sensitive to capital upgrade costs and remaining useful lifetime of the facility after upgrade (Fig. 4a–d). LVOF is most sensitive to capital upgrade costs at facilities with lower loads and/or more constrained energy discharge capacities, as highlighted in the water distribution case. As expected, upgrading facilities near the end of their useful life diminishes the LVOF. The LVOF can also exhibit small decreases at facilities with long expected lifespans as a result of the method discounting future benefits from flexible load operation over long time periods. The break-even line (LVOF = 0) and the battery equivalency lines are highly specific to the water asset. For example, the advanced water treatment with curtailment case shows NPV-positive investments over a large range of lifetimes with capital upgrade costs in the millions of dollars, whereas the water distribution case is only NPV positive for very small capital upgrade costs. For the wastewater treatment and water distribution cases, installing a 1-LHE battery has a higher NPV than flexible load operation because these systems lack native flexibility (that is, tight time-varying production constraints) or have highly variable Nature Water | Volume 2 | October 2024 | 1028–1037 **1032** **Analysis** https://doi.org/10.1038/s44221-024-00316-4 **==> picture [522 x 444] intentionally omitted <==** **----- Start of picture text -----**

**Fig. 3 | Energy performance metrics. a** – **c** , Load profiles for an electricity cost-optimal flexible operating schema in advanced water treatment ( **a** ), water supply distribution ( **b** ) and wastewater treatment systems ( **c** ) are shown over a sample summer weekday. The baseline system operations are optimized based a constant electricity rate, whereas the flexible system operations are subject to a rate structure that resembles utility rates from Santa Barbara Clean Energy[36] . The peak electricity price hours (16:00–21:00) are highlighted in grey. **d** – **f** , The normalized, annual round-trip efficiency, energy capacity and power capacity associated with the Santa Barbara Clean Energy electricity tariff structure are shown. The energy capacity is normalized based on total baseline energy consumption. The power capacity is normalized based on the average baseline power consumption. **g** – **i** , Sensitivities on the energy performance metrics for each system are conducted with simulations using summer (July), spring (April) and winter (December) utility rate structures from Houston, New York, San Jose, Santa Barbara and Tampa[36] . These cities were selected to represent a diverse range of tariff structures and average electricity prices. The round-trip efficiency is not defined for the supply curtailment scenario as it does not complete a round trip. All cases of desalination supply curtailment assume the baseline equivalent of 5 h of lost water production, which may be representative of typical demandresponse events. baseline loads. Past work has also demonstrated synergistic benefits between flexible load operation and battery storage at wastewater treatment facilities, where coordinating battery charging with process operations can increase biogas utilization and further enhance facility flexibility[12] . By contrast, assets where the baseline operation is near steady state derive little added value from an on-site battery. Flexible load operation of advanced water treatment is notably more cost effective than steady state operation with battery storage for most cases. The LVOF of an asset is also highly sensitive to the magnitude and timing of price variation within a tariff structure (Fig. 4e). Facilities operating under highly time variant tariff structures (that is, high peak–off-peak ratio; PG&E and Santa Barbara Clean Energy) are more strongly incentivized to operate flexibly. Even so, the timing of the tariff structure’s peak and off-peak intervals must align with the timing of an asset’s native flexibility to realize a high LVOF. None of the examined assets can fully shift their load out of the peak price period due to system constraints such as water treatment, water delivery or tank storage limitations (Fig. 3a–c). LVOF is a valuable metric for unifying the comparison of savings from flexibility across and outside of the water sector; however if the NPV or ROI is too low, facilities may decide not to deploy native flexibility (Fig. 4f). **Fig. 4 | Levelized value of flexibility. a** – **d** , The LVOF is displayed for each of the systems described in Fig. 3, using the Santa Barbara Clean Energy electricity tariff structure. The net upgrade costs represent the costs associated with facilities upgrades that enable operational flexibility (for example, a new control system or software update) and are approximated by ranges in literature[49][,][50] . The range of capital upgrade cost is extended to represent unit process changes that may be required for an advanced water treatment to restart quickly after a shutdown event. The facility lifetime represents the number of years remaining in the facility’s economic lifetime or the years over which the capital investment upgrades will be amortized. The solid isoline represents the equilibrium space for NPV-positive upgrades. The dashed isoline represents the value of installing a 1-load-hour-equivalent lithium-ion battery at a capital cost of US$450 kWh[−1] (ref. 48). In the water distribution and wastewater treatment cases ( **c** , **d** ), the battery contour line is not present on the chart but referenced within the colour scale and fully labelled in **d** . **e** , **f** , Consider a sensitivity of LVOF to tariff rate structures and asset classes assuming a net capital upgrade cost of US$0 (ref. 36). In these cases where native flexibility is considered (capital upgrade cost = $0), return on investment is undefined. All cases of desalination supply curtailment assume the baseline equivalent of 5 h of lost water production, which is representative of typical demand-response events across tariffs. Advanced water treatment under the Houston tariff structure is not shown due to negligible differences between the flexible and baseline operating schedules. Fig. 4f has a competitive LVOF, but since the asset has a much smaller energy load, the magnitude of lifetime savings is low. In contrast, the NPV of the wastewater treatment case is the largest across the examined cases but yields the lowest LVOF due to the large absolute energy capacity. Whereas native flexibility has high NPV in this case, the LVOF metric allows the operator to determine that installing a battery would be a much more valuable investment. Finally, the existing asset configurations modelled above are often constrained in their flexibility by one or more components (for example, inadequate capacity, no intermediate water storage, static pumps). To alleviate these facility-level constraints, capital improvement plans might include investments to enhance energy flexibility. Facility upgrades that unlock new energy or power capacity will often have LVOF values that are not readily extrapolated from the above plots. In some cases, such as those where there is a great deal of native flexibility in the system, there are diminishing marginal returns from additional flexibility. In other cases, enhanced flexibility is expected to be super linear because of newly imparted ability to avoid high demand charges. Water system operators would benefit from additional scholarship exploring the specific constraints on flexibility at facilities and developing supply curves for energy flexibility upgrades based on technology options and optimized operations. An understanding of a facility’s capacity-cost relationship for energy flexibility, which will vary change seasonally with capacity requirements, is key in determining the frequency at which flexible loads may be used and their bulk effects on the grid over longer timescales. ## **Outlook** Realizing the grid benefits of water asset flexibility requires a common framework for valuing key metrics across diverse electricity and water infrastructure systems. The proposed framework characterizes the value of flexible load energy services to the grid and the value of those services to water facility operators seeking to minimize growing Nature Water | Volume 2 | October 2024 | 1028–1037 **1034** **Analysis** https://doi.org/10.1038/s44221-024-00316-4 **==> picture [521 x 151] intentionally omitted <==** **----- Start of picture text -----**
